TextENGNew: "Hey, Nakagawa-kun!@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1002. It's been ages!@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1003. Still keeping at it?@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1004. You know, that thing.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1005. Waaahahahahahaha!"
"Oh no, not at all anymore.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_nakagawa1002. Getting old is no fun."
"You have to stay focused when you work with clay.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1007. If you leave it too long, you lose your touch, and then what's the point?"
"Sensei, allow me to introduce someone.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_nakagawa1004. This is Major Takano."
"Pleased to meet you.@k@vS20/09/VTK1_takano1055. Thank you for making time in your busy schedule to see me today."
"Oh, I didn't make time for you specifically.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1009. I was told there would be fine fresh sea urchin!
Sea urchin is wonderful, you know—rich in vitamin A, excellent for the skin.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1011. Women love sea urchin, don't they?@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1012. Waaahahahahahaha!!"
I'll concede that sea urchin is rich in vitamin A, but compared to something like spinach, it's hardly worth making a fuss over.
...Well, none of that mattered right now.
He just struck me as an unpleasant man, and I couldn't resist the urge to find fault.
Today's men were directors of the Alphabet Project, the body that controlled the Irie Institute's funding.
Or people positioned to influence those directors.
...Put simply, they were the Irie Institute's new masters.
Though none of them carried the weight Uncle Koizumi once had, they were still leaders and power brokers across various spheres.
I had to be extremely careful not to offend them...
Given who they were, there was no way someone of my standing could have summoned them.
It was only by calling in every remaining favor from the old Koizumi faction's network that I had managed to arrange this meeting.
Uncle Koizumi, who had moved heaven and earth to make everything go my way, was no longer here.
From here on, I truly had to carve my own path—alone.
But today's stage wasn't mine alone.
I couldn't let the people who had arranged this meeting lose face, so I had to wait quietly, pouring drinks, until my turn came.
My primary objective today was to explain what an extraordinary research subject Hinamizawa Syndrome was, and to request an indefinite extension of our research.
These men only saw money. They had no conception of the potential that Hinamizawa Syndrome held.
They failed to understand that it warranted such a substantial research investment.
This research wasn't limited to the mere existence of an unknown parasite—it had the potential to overturn the very foundations of anthropology.
Even a Nobel Prize wouldn't suffice to honor it.
...Explaining this to men who knew nothing about the subject would be difficult, but it had to be done.
To that end, I had spent considerable time preparing clear, accessible materials that wouldn't leave laypeople lost in jargon.
changeReason: New translation adds detail about not confusing laypeople with jargon, matching the JPN 煙に巻く (to baffle/mislead), which the old translation omitted.

TextENGNew: We researchers sometimes deliberately obfuscate our documents because we dislike engaging with the uninformed, but that wasn't an option this time.
I had to set aside any condescension and genuinely seek their understanding.
The result was an enormous pile of straightforward documentation.
...Truthfully, I resented wasting time over leisurely kaiseki cuisine.
I wanted to cut to the point and explain everything in painstaking detail until they understood.
...But there's a saying about the beggar who rushes getting less.
changeReason: Different proverbs used: old uses 'luck lies in the last helping', new uses 'beggar who rushes getting less'; the new translation is a more literal rendering of the JPN proverb 慌てる乞食はもらいが少ない.

TextENGNew: ...For now, I could only wait patiently.

"Hmm.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1014. Honestly, I never thought much of the old Alphabet Project.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1015. In the end, it was nothing but a sandbox for old men with their heads still stuck in the war."
"A sandbox for old men to dream of resurrecting Imperial Japan with other people's money.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiB1002. Do you have any idea how much public funding was squandered on those delusions?"
"From what I hear, people like Okuno-sensei have been prodding the prosecutors to go after the old board for embezzlement."
changeReason: Subject reversed: JPN says Okuno-sensei is prodding prosecutors (奥野先生あたりが検察を突っついて), but old says 'prosecutors poking people like Okuno-sensei.'

TextENGNew: "Waahahahahahahaha.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1017. That's what they get for sucking up to those old men and riding their coattails.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1018. Of course, none of it can be made public, so they'll probably be forced to resign or get demoted on some unrelated pretext.

...Well, this is what Japan gets for letting the ghosts of prewar ambition run free all these years!@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1020. Ah, these grilled sea urchins are divine!@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1021. Waaahahahahahaha!"
changeReason: Old says 'ghosts of the war', new says 'ghosts of prewar ambition'; the JPN 戦前の亡霊 specifically means 'prewar ghosts', so the new translation is more accurate.

TextENGNew: "That's rather harsh...@k@vS20/00/VTK1_nakagawa1006. I realize it's extremely awkward to bring this up under the circumstances, but...@k@vS20/00/VTK1_nakagawa1007. might I have a moment of your time?"

"Ah, you're from the Irie Institute, aren't you?@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiB1004. Doing research on some local disease out in Nagano or Gifu, was it?@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiB1005. Uhh, what was the situation again?"
"The board decided to halt all military application research.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_nakagawa1009. The research program itself is also set to wind down within three years.
...Oh, no, sorry—that's still under discussion.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_nakagawa1011. Either way, the facility is scheduled to close within a few years."

"Ah, yes!!@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiC1003. Now I remember.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiC1004. The Irie Institute had a bad reputation even under the old board.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiC1005. After all, it was draining enormous sums with virtually no oversight."
"Some bigwig was pushing hard for it for reasons no one quite understood.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1023. Because of that, nobody could say a word, and it was left unchecked."
"...But you know,@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiB1007. that bigwig had quite the reputation with women, so perhaps that had something to do with it—wahahahahahaha!!"
Was he implying that Uncle Koizumi couldn't tell the difference between public and private affairs?
changeReason: Old fabricates sexual implication ('slept with Uncle Koizumi'); JPN says 公私混同の区別もつかない (couldn't distinguish public from private affairs) — entirely different and much more severe meaning.

TextENGNew: ...It was nothing less than an insult to both of us.
...But I held my tongue.
changeReason: Old says 'held my anger in', new says 'held my tongue'; different actions — restraining emotion vs. restraining speech. JPN ぐっと堪える means to endure/restrain oneself, both are valid but convey different restraint.

TextENGNew: I couldn't afford to let something like this show on my face.
"...In any case, today I have taken the liberty of requesting your time to explain the details of our Institute's research, and to respectfully ask that the board's decision be reconsidered.
I am deeply grateful that you have taken time from your busy schedules to attend today."
"Well, I suppose free sea urchins don't come without strings attached!@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1025. Waahahahahahaha!"
I explained the significance of Hinamizawa Syndrome's existence, taking care not to rush.
I tried to convey that the syndrome's potential extended far beyond something as narrow as military application—that it held implications vastly more profound.
...That it might explain ideology and religion themselves.
That it might shake the very definition of what it means to be human.
I took my time, explaining slowly and carefully.
I had no confidence that all of it had gotten through to these boorish men, but even partial understanding would suffice.
...As long as they grasped what a monumental discovery Hinamizawa Syndrome truly was...
"Thank you, Major.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1027. ...Hmmm, I see.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1028. ...Hmm."
"We'd like to discuss this amongst ourselves, so could the Major please step out?"
"...Of course.@k@vS20/09/VTK1_takano1059. Please excuse me."
changeReason: 'Yes/Thank you' vs 'Of course/Please excuse me' — the JPN 失礼させていただきます means 'I'll take my leave', which the new translation captures more accurately.

TextENGNew: Naturally, they couldn't deliberate with me present.
...I had left all the documents with them.
All I could do now was hope they would view them favorably.
I left the room and slid the door closed.
...There was a waiting room next door, and I was meant to go there.
But I stopped.
...It was a breach of etiquette, but I pressed my ear to the sliding door and listened...
"Well... wahahahaha, how shall I put this?@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1030. I feel sorry for the previous board members, honestly."
"...Indeed, hahahaha.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_nakagawa1013. Koizumi-sensei was pushing this so forcefully..."
"I met him once, when he became the founding adviser to the Lowell Pharmaceuticals Japan subsidiary. He struck me as an exceptionally sharp man... Hmmm..."
changeReason: Old translates founding 'director' vs new's 'adviser'; JPN 顧問 means adviser/consultant, not director — the new translation corrects this.

TextENGNew: "You just can't win against age, I suppose!@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiC1007. It really makes you appreciate the importance of knowing when to bow out."
"That said, it is a fact that this very rare disease called Hinamizawa Syndrome exists.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_nakagawa1015. Not everything here is pure fantasy..."
"But the Irie Institute has already identified the disease and developed a treatment, haven't they?@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiB1011. Isn't that sufficient?"
"In modern Japan, we must exercise great caution regarding matters that could unsettle neighboring nations.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiC1009. Military affairs are the most sensitive of all.
At the very least, the fact that military-oriented research was conducted for several years must be expunged immediately."
"Is that separate from simply discontinuing the military research?"
"Yes.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiC1012. Even if we stop immediately, the fact that Hinamizawa Syndrome was being researched for the purpose of developing biological weapons remains.

The fact that research was conducted on Hinamizawa Syndrome, for any purpose, must be kept under the strictest secrecy.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiC1014. Ideally, Hinamizawa Syndrome itself would simply cease to exist."
changeReason: Old says 'hide that fact no matter what purpose', new says 'kept under the strictest secrecy'; also old says 'Hinamizawa Syndrome just disappears' vs new's 'would simply cease to exist' — the new adds 'for any purpose' matching JPN 目的は何であれ more precisely.

TextENGNew: "Precisely.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiB1013. If the disease persists, sooner or later someone will take an interest and dig up what we've buried.
Following the board's decision, once the Irie Institute has eradicated the disease, they should destroy all evidence and disband."
changeReason: Old attributes the suggestion to the narrator ('I think we should follow'), new presents it as an impersonal recommendation ('Following the board's decision'); the JPN is a suggestion in formal speech, new is more accurate.

TextENGNew: "Hmm.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1032. That does seem to be the inevitable conclusion.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1033. It's not even a question of Nobel Prizes. This research is simply unnecessary for postwar Japan."

"Besides, this disease has existed since ancient times, and we've gotten by without any particular problems.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1035. From the start, I've thought this amounted to nothing more than poking at a hornet's nest."
"I agree.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiB1016. I don't doubt the syndrome exists, but this Dr. Takano—the one who supposedly discovered it—has embellished things beyond all reason!"
"He certainly had a... unique perspective.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiB1018. I've never heard of a parasite capable of influencing human thought.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiB1019. That's simply absurd."
changeReason: Old incorrectly refers to Dr. Takano as 'He'; Takano (高野) here is the grandfather, but the congressman uses 第一発見者 (first discoverer). The new translation also adds 'supposedly' with 'the one who supposedly discovered it', adding skepticism matching the tone.

TextENGNew: "If there are parasites that live in animal brains, I suppose it's conceivable that some might live in human brains as well.
But for them to control human thoughts and personality? Wahahahahaha!@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiC1017. That's a bit too 'unique' for me."
changeReason: Old says 'control human thoughts' vs new adds 'and personality'; JPN 思想や人格 means 'thoughts and personality', so new is more complete.

TextENGNew: ...Peering through the gap in the sliding door, I was seized by an overwhelming sense of déjà vu.
changeReason: Old says 'unbelievable sense of deja vu' vs new says 'overwhelming sense of deja vu'; also 'illusion' vs JPN-matching 'deja vu'. Additionally, new uses the proper accent on 'déjà vu'.

TextENGNew: This should have been my first time experiencing this, yet... it didn't feel that way at all.
I didn't understand why, but the memory was unbearably sad and bitter... Wait... what...?
changeReason: Old says 'memory is so sad and so painful' vs new says 'unbearably sad and bitter'; JPN 苦々しくて means 'bitter', not 'painful' — different emotional quality.

TextENGNew: Why... are there tears...?
Before I knew it, the room beyond the door was no longer tatami—it had become a room with a deeply familiar carpet.
An odd but deeply comforting smell of dust mingled with the sharp scent of some chemical reagent wafted from within.
changeReason: Different meaning: old says 'smell...but it's very soothing', new restructures as 'odd but deeply comforting smell...wafted from within'; the new version treats the description differently, with the smell wafting out rather than filling the room.

TextENGNew: And there... was the achingly familiar figure of my grandfather.
My grandfather, who had poured his heart into his thesis only to have it savaged... who wasn't even permitted to shed a tear.
This was no illusion.
...After all, the documents I had entrusted to them today contained numerous citations from my grandfather's thesis.
...So it was no wonder that my grandfather was standing there, explaining his work to them himself.
...As tears fell for the beloved image of my grandfather, I watched these men mock and belittle his life's work...
I had seen this scene before.
And it should have ended there...
Yet somehow, the same scene was being replayed before my eyes...
All I could do was stare blankly through the sliding door at this strange world where reality and memory overlapped.

"That Major is a bit peculiar,@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1037. and Koizumi-sensei, who believed such nonsense, was peculiar too...@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1038. but the strangest of all is the person who wrote this thesis in the first place."
"It's a jumble of hopes and fantasies—this reads more like fiction than a thesis.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiB1021. Honestly, I think it'd be a bestseller if someone published it as a novel."
"Absolutely!@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1040. I have a friend who runs a publishing house.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1041. I bet he'd get a real kick out of it!@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1042. Wahahahahahaha!!"
"This is no laughing matter, gentlemen.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiC1019. A staggering amount of money has been poured into this for years.
The most alarming part is that extremely dangerous equipment has been procured as a contingency measure.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiC1021. The public must never learn of this."
changeReason: Old says 'equipment used to gas the villagers'; new says 'extremely dangerous equipment as a contingency measure'. The JPN 相当危険な機材 means 'extremely dangerous equipment', and the old translation adds specificity about gassing that isn't in the original.

TextENGNew: "The very existence of such a plan is terrifying enough, but if anyone found out about this equipment, we'd turn not only international opinion but domestic opinion against us.
changeReason: Old says 'if people find out about this equipment, the whole country will turn against us'; new says 'we'd turn not only international opinion but domestic opinion against us'. The JPN mentions 国外はおろか国内世論も (not just foreign but also domestic public opinion), which the old translation loses.

TextENGNew: And on top of that, the maintenance costs for this equipment are astronomical!!"
"You don't really think all that money is going to maintenance, do you?@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiB1023. It's obviously a shell project being used to funnel funds.
And what was it again?@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiC1023. One girl dies and the entire village goes berserk? What comic book did that come from?"

"Exactly!@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiB1025. That anyone could swallow such a childish fairy tale and pour enormous sums into it—now that is truly insane!@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiB1026. The fact that people actually took these documents seriously is beyond ridiculous!"
Saying that, he set down the documents with an exaggerated gesture.
...Set down is too generous—slammed would be more accurate.
Because the bundle of papers didn't land neatly; they scattered across the floor.
"You said it—wahahahahaha!!@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1046. Ah, if you'll excuse me.@k@vS20/00/VTK1_daigiA1047. I need to use the restroom."
The man stood up... and stepped squarely on the papers.
They weren't the papers on the tatami... but the ones on the carpet. My grandfather's thesis.
changeReason: New adds identification: 'They weren't the papers on the tatami... but the ones on the carpet. My grandfather's thesis.' The old omits 'My grandfather's thesis' which is implied in the JPN 祖父の論文.

TextENGNew: There stood my grandfather, his heart ready to burst with grief.
And yet he couldn't let it show on his face.
Grandfather's expression didn't appear to change.
...But to me... it looked distorted.
I could clearly see the walls of my grandfather's study buckling and warping behind him in sorrow.
I burst into the room.
...And threw myself at the old gentleman's legs.
It wasn't that I hated him.
Well, if asked whether I hated him, of course I did... but that wasn't the point. That wasn't it at all.
I simply could not forgive the foot that was trampling my grandfather's thesis.
"Don't step on them...!!@k@vS20/09/VTK1_takano1062. Please, don't step on them...!!@k@vS20/09/VTK1_takano1063. My grandpa worked so hard to write those... please don't step on them...!!"
changeReason: Old says 'Don't!!/Don't step on them!!/ My grandpa wrote these. Please don't step on them!!!' vs new says 'Don't step on them...!!/Please, don't step on them...!!/My grandpa worked so hard to write those... please don't step on them...!!' — the new adds 'worked so hard' matching JPN 頑張って書いた and has a significantly different emotional tone with pleading rather than commanding.

TextENGNew: "...Oh, uh... excuse me..."
His foot was still grinding down on the papers. I wanted to pull them free.
...But his legs were planted like they'd grown roots, and no amount of pushing or pulling from someone like me could budge them.
So I clung to his legs and begged.
I tried to pull the thesis out from under his sole.
But I couldn't move his foot. I couldn't free the papers...
"Don't step on them... please, don't step on them...@k@vS20/09/VTK1_takano1065. Grandpa's... don't... don't step on them..."
In the deathly silent room, only the sound of my sobbing echoed.
...Just as it had that day...
